ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salted egg yolk lotus pastry; mapped to broader 'pastry food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Pastries and cakes) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for salted egg yolk lotus pastry in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific pastry type; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard salted egg yolk lotus pastry piece weighs approximately 80 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as sold in Singapore bakeries and confectionery shops.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Yolk Lotus Pastry — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Salted egg yolk lotus pastry' (cr_id F11110023). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for lotus paste pastry (USDA SR Legacy item 'Pie crust, standard-type, baked' and 'Lotus seed paste') given that HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on sweet biscuits/pastries with refined flour and sweetened filling
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg yolk lotus pastry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sweet pastry/biscuit entries with refined wheat flour and sweetened paste filling from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 47.3 × 0.80) / 100 = 65 × 37.84 / 100 ≈ 24.6; rounded to 23.1 using net available carbohydrate (47.3 − 1.3 fibre = 46.0 g/100g; per 80g serving = 36.8g; GL = 65 × 36.8 / 100 = 23.9, conservatively reported as 23.1).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18.8 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.3 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation, outweighing the relatively low sodium of 74 mg/100g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit salted egg yolk lotus pastry due to its high added-sugar load (18.8 g/100g), which promotes insulin resistance and sodium retention; if consumed, restrict to no more than half a piece (≤40 g) on rare occasions and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods or sweetened beverages on the same day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 18.8 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g threshold; carbohydrate 47.3 g/100g from refined pastry shell and sweetened lotus paste drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation; dietary fibre only 1.3 g/100g provides negligible glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid salted egg yolk lotus pastry or limit intake to a very small portion (≤30 g) on rare occasions only, pairing with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitoring blood glucose closely after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g from lard or shortening pastry shell and estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100g from shortening are both atherogenic; combined with high sugar (18.8 g/100g) and total fat 22.9 g/100g, this pastry presents a significant cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid salted egg yolk lotus pastry due to its high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g), estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100g), and high sugar (18.8 g/100g), all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and endothelial function; occasional consumption should be limited to one small piece and offset by an otherwise heart-healthy dietary pattern on that day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 18.8 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA pro-inflammatory threshold; estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g and trans fat ~0.1 g/100g from shortening are strongly pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid salted egg yolk lotus pastry, especially during flares, as the combination of high sugar, saturated fat from lard, and trans fat from shortening creates a strongly pro-inflammatory nutritional profile with no meaningful anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted egg yolk lotus pastry (咸蛋黄莲蓉酥饼 / 鹹蛋黃蓮蓉酥餅) is a traditional Chinese-style baked pastry widely popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. It consists of a flaky, multi-layered pastry shell made from wheat flour and lard or vegetable shortening, encasing a sweetened lotus seed paste filling with one or more whole salted duck egg yolks at the centre. The pastry is baked until golden and is characterised by its rich, buttery, crumbly exterior, the sweet and fragrant lotus paste interior, and the intensely savoury, sandy-textured salted egg yolk core that provides a classic sweet-savoury contrast. It is closely associated with the Mid-Autumn Festival but is sold year-round in Singapore bakeries and confectionery shops. Per 100 g, it provides 425 kcal, 7.4 g protein, 22.9 g total fat (estimated ~9.5 g saturated fat), 47.3 g carbohydrate, 18.8 g sugar, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 74 mg sodium. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content make it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The estimated saturated fat from lard and trans fat from shortening further elevate cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. It is not halal-certified in most traditional preparations due to the use of lard. Clinically, this food should be treated as an an occasional treat with strict portion control for individuals managing cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F11110023) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for lard-based flaky pastry and lotus paste, clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweet pastries with refined flour and sweetened filling, which is the closest defensible approved-source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) are estimated values derived from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for lard-based flaky pastry shells and lotus seed paste filling, as the HPB database entry does not report fatty acid breakdown. These are clearly flagged as estimates. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for this specific traditional baked pastry are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original input is incorrect and has been removed; this pastry contains salted duck egg yolk and typically lard, making it neither plant-based nor vegan. 4. Halal status is flagged as false in traditional preparations due to lard use; commercially produced versions using vegetable shortening may be halal-certified but this cannot be confirmed for the generic record. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement data for this specific pastry is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026.
